## [03:00 - 06:00]

So the way I manage that is to just slowly start creeping up the budgets for your retargeting, for example, or your warm audience retargeting based on like if you're doing increases in your budget for cold and you seem more traffic come through, then at that point, just bump up your budgets for retargeting and things like that. But what you want to do is make sure that you're doing in really small increments and then measuring whether your CPN ROAS changes massively. And if it does, there's a chance that it's down to frequency. So unfortunately, there's no right or wrong with frequency. I had an e-commerce client where when we took it over, I was like, you know, your frequency is way too high. And I can see that performance is good. But what happened is that account works best when the frequency is high. So we were looking at like weekly periods of frequencies of some retargeting ad sets were up to 12, if not 16 impressions in a week. Daily was something like two, three, even four, especially because a lot of these were fans as well. And you hit them up a lot more. But it was working. And so what we actually started to do was cycle creative. So like for example, if you've got creative that's working in your smaller ad sets, but they're generating really good CPA and you see that performance starts to drop, pulls it out, load another one, give it a chance to start working. And obviously, if you've tested through graduation, you'll know that it's a good creative. And then just cycle through the ads. So one of the things I'm looking at the moment is automating this whole process. The problem is knowing when to replace. And so I'm kind of looking at frequency as a guide, but really looking at CPN ROAS that that's still happening and that's still performing. The other thing is generally, I would also look at the negative feedback and the positive feedback. I don't know if you caught the video yesterday, but Facebook are taking that away in July, which I think is a big, big, big shame. But that would also tell you if you're hitting too many people and you're annoying people and the negative feedback is too high, then you've got a bigger problem there. And the problem there is if you get too much negative feedback on your ads, that will have a detrimental impact on the ad set as well. You can have a situation where you've just maxed out your frequencies, you've annoyed so many people and then you get penalized in the auction as well. So frequency is important, but primarily I'm looking at CPN ROAS to determine if that performance is good or not at the ad level and then frequency just to kind of make a call. At the same time, keeping on relevant score as well. So when you are seeing good performance, you know what their relevant score is, whether it's a four or an eight. If you start to see that drop and frequency go up, then I would pause the ad and fire up a new one because you know it's going in the wrong direction. So that's on kind of the frequencies and budgets and things like that. So yeah, I wouldn't necessarily stop an ad and just not use it. I try and catch it before it gets really bad, relevant score or the frequencies go too high and stuff and just pause it out, maybe even just pause it for three days and then restart

## [06:00 - 09:04]

it again. Because what's happening is people are seeing that in their news feed and they're not reacting to it, whereas some people are and this is the challenge you've got. So if your retargeting pot is let's say 5,000 people and CPA is really good, but your frequency is high and your relevant score is dropping, it means the people that are going to react to that ad have reacted and they've purchased, but there's a group of people that are not and so you just keep cycling your creatives within that and trying to capture different people with different ads as well. Is there ever a time where you turn off entirely a warm or a hot portion of the funnel or is it always running? I mean it's always running. And then I think one part that I'm still not sure of is when you're looking, what kind of timeframe are you looking at? I mean, are you always looking at lifetime or is it like, are you adjusting it? That depends on the purchase cycle. So one of my clients is like they purchase mostly within five to seven days. I'm looking at that period. If you've got a low ticket item, you know, AOV of $20 to $40, then probably the purchase will be within one or two days. So I'm looking at the frequency in that period. And then just what I would do is if your purchase is within the one to three day period, then for me, if my retargeting pot is big enough, then I put most of the budget between the one and three days and then reduce the budget down. So I'm not over hitting the people that I know are not generally going to convert. There will be a few conversions that triple through, but that's how I'm kind of managing the budget there to get as much profit, but also try and squeeze more out of the other people as well. So yeah, just on the graduation framework, is that actually just to clarify, can you use that? I've been running for about two to three years. I've got quite mature picks. So is it still worthwhile to test that in the background as it were alongside the stuff that you're already running? Like, you said more as a kind of getting started type of approach. I just wondered if I should actually kind of have a little bit of testing budget alongside what I'm currently running as I've been running for a couple of years. I just wondered what your thoughts were on that. The short answer is absolutely yes. The me testing never stops and the graduation method, it allows you to be testing in the background regardless of what budget you want to set aside. So there's three parts of what I generally test in the graduation framework is creative audience and products or offer. And you're going to always be testing one of those things. Creative, for example, you're always going to want to know if I change this image, is it better or if I try this copy or if I do this approach, is it going to be better? So that's where the graduation framework allows you to just have that constantly running in the background. Because what I always want to do is I don't want to be in a situation where I need a creative

## [09:04 - 12:08]

because performance has dropped and then we are rushing around with the design team and doing things like that. I'm always testing the background. If your audience is a stable, if you're not really doing too much with your products or new offers, then at the very least, keep on testing new creative. It doesn't have to be more than five or 10 pounds a day. You can just have your best ad set, your best audience, sorry, with different creatives and you're just testing those in the background. So if your design team are, for example, delivering one new asset a week and you can create a few variants of it, just have it running in the background. So UK 5% look alike plus UK broad, no interest layering or no look alike. And similar with the US 1% and broad. Is it worthwhile kind of bringing in some interest again to test interest or would you say that's probably not that useful once you've got a good pixel data? Yes, one of the, if it's worth me actually getting back into interest or not. So if you're getting good performance from a broad audience, then you probably don't need interest if your CPAs or IRS is good. It's more if you feel like you're going to have to stretch to different audiences. So from the sounds of it, if you've got 5% look alike and your broad pixels working, I wouldn't massively focus on interests. For me, I reckon that you're going to get more performance from creative testing, trying different formats, different angles and things like that to try and cut through different audiences and also test running video views alongside your WC campaign, for example. So you can reach more people, you can prime up more people with your video ads and then you can test the video ads retargeting audience as well. So that's probably how I'd approach that. So kind of a PPE and a video views to set two additional campaigns or... Yes, a PPE is more just for testing, but the video view is just to expand your audiences out. So you'll go through it in the graduation framework. I talk about the kind of Venn diagram of different audiences and how you can have one big audience. It could be a million or 10 million. The website conversion is just one part of that audience. So that's where it's good to overlap with video views just to get a wider audience. We're doing this with a client right now where video views is actually bringing back a really good cost proposition. And the reason is that Facebook is trying to predict that these are your buyers, they're your website conversion and objective people and the people that are likely to purchase. But guaranteed there's a lot of people in your wider audience that you're probably not reaching because Facebook is just going after the best users.

## [24:21 - 27:23]

sure. Sure. Yeah. Cool. I'm Keon. Hey, yeah, I think it's kind of a maybe a slightly similar question might Jeff, I raised earlier. But I'm currently virtually scaling some of my good ad sets at the moment, looking at like 20% a day, if performance continues. Yeah. But you know, I'm just thinking ahead, like, you know, if I keep doing 20%, my body's going to get quite quite large quite quickly. Do you, you know, and if you think about that further into a few months, you know, you could get quite big. So I'm just wondering if there's a almost like a cap, like, you know, when do you stop increasing or when do you reduce that percentage, potentially if your budget's getting towards your thousands or just thinking about, you know, especially into Q4, where I know it's going to get quite quite big as well. I'm just thinking about, you know, how at what point do I say, okay, the performance is dips enough to want me to maybe hold it here for for a few days, and then maybe start doing more increases, or do I even cut back potentially depending on, you know, if my last three days CPA is kind of a bit off mark, for example, yeah, would I then reduce down the butt start reducing down to find a better level. So I just wondered what I think the tactics were around, you know, when to stop and and if to stop or when to cut back a bit more detail and that would be useful. So that's definitely in the training videos. So there's part of the scaling part of the program. There's something I call the staircase scaling strategy. And it's not just about infinitely increasing your budget. One of the things I share is that the auction looks at punishment and reward. So reward for an answer is when you increase budget because that goes back as a signal to Facebook that says things are working well, this guy's happy, I'm going to continue increasing looking at this audience and performance and things like that. But the inverse is true as well that when you reduce your budget down from that set, Facebook will readjust itself and try and find a better audience. So you're telling Facebook, you're not quite doing a good job here. And so that they're both strategies I would use together. So when I'm scaling upwards, so vertically, I generally do 20% a day or twice a day. But I would also reduce budgets by 10% as well, when the performance isn't good. So the compound impact of that is always going to be a positive. So if you increase 20%, you decrease 10%. You've still got a net positive. If you do two 20% increments, one 10% decrement is still on a net positive as well. Because I just want to give the bad signal back to Facebook. If you're seeing really bad performance, then obviously you might want to decrease it a lot more. So where we talk about not increasing budgets more than 20% ago, if you're decreasing it, you decrease it to a level you're comfortable with. So if you've gone from 500 a day to 2,500 a day and things are going well and all of a sudden they go bad, then you might want to cut it by 500, 1000 or whatever it be to get you back to that comfort

## [27:23 - 30:25]

level where you can get good performance. What I'm looking for is, so when it comes to cost back position, there's two things that are in play, which is conversion rate and the cost per click. Generally, just scaling upwards, your cost per click will also increase as well because Facebook's trying to spend your money in different places and it's testing all these different areas out as well. And you can either wait for Facebook to find its level. So if you do a 20% bump and in the day you see, see if you guys go up, generally they'll even out and then they'll get back to the level it should be and it becomes a waiting game. If you don't see it come back in 24 hours, then that's definitely not a good signal. You might want to reduce budget or refresh your creative. But if you bump it 20% and CPA still maintains, then personally I would bump it again, but that's something you have to be really comfortable with. And that's why I talk about mindset as well. So I take a lot more risks with scaling because I'm confident of identifying the signals and knowing when to take the shift. And this comes with practice as well. So for someone that's starting out, I recommend doing 20% every other day. So it gives the algorithm time to just settle and then you do another bump. But on your question, I wouldn't not decrease if you're seeing back performance. I'd absolutely decrease it down and try and bring that CPA back out as well. Yeah, sounds good. So what I call horizontal scaling. So you'll see that in the training video. And there's pros and cons of doing both approaches. ## [30:25 - 33:25]

them with the same ad? What's your sort of methods? My method would be to test that because you don't know if they've had enough touch points to really get it. So they might have seen your ad once in a cold audience clicked into your website. Now all of a sudden they're in retargeting so they might not have progressed far enough down the funnel to be more in the kind of consideration stage. So they might still need something similar to just nudge them. So if you are doing a website retargeting then I would probably split out those that have viewed the product page from those that have not viewed the product page because if they haven't got down to the product page for me they're still cold because they haven't really taken that next step. They're not warmed up until they've actually seen the product then obviously if they've added to carp then that's a hot prospect. So that's probably the way I'd use it and then just test the different messages out and see if you can use the same kind of messaging or theme as you're called that you've hit for the kind of people that have not visited your website. Maybe just try shifting it up for those that have visited website as well. I've seen some accounts where they actually bundle cold and website visitors into the same so they don't retarget website visitors until they hit the product stage because they know that at that point that's worth retargeting. So it's not always a good thing to just retarget people that visit your website because you can get a lot of trash within that as well. Another way of doing retargeting is doing your top 25% audience and that way if you're doing has visited your website but is the top 25% by profile and Facebook will recognize that as people that are engaged on the site more than other people. That's also a good way of doing it so if you've seen some of the recommendations over the last year or so where people say delay the pixel fire and remove bots and things like that I'd absolutely not do that but if you are trying to eliminate those bounces then go for your top 25% or 50% website visitors and use that for your targeting.
